serving.natural_language_portion: One serving of Matcha Mochimochi is typically a single donut, weighing approximately 70 grams.

nutrient_source.title: Doughnut, cake-type, plain
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values adapted from USDA FoodData Central for 'Doughnut, cake-type, plain' and adjusted for frosting and matcha content based on typical ingredient profiles. Original input values were used as a guide where reasonable.

gi_source.title: Doughnut, cake-type
gi_source.source_match_notes: Glycemic index estimated from 'Doughnut, cake-type' (GI 76) from the University of Sydney GI Database, as mochi donuts, despite their chewy texture, are primarily made from refined flours and sugar, similar to traditional cake donuts. The high sugar content from frosting further contributes to a high GI.

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar and saturated fat content, deep-fried preparation
hypertension.guidance: This food is not suitable for individuals with hypertension due to its high sugar (25.7g/100g) and saturated fat (11.4g/100g) content, which can contribute to increased blood pressure and arterial stiffness.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very high sugar and carbohydrate content, low fiber, high glycemic index
type2_diabetes.guidance: Matcha Mochimochi is highly unsuitable for Type 2 Diabetes management due to its very high sugar (25.7g/100g) and carbohydrate (42.9g/100g) content, low dietary fiber (1.4g/100g), and high estimated glycemic index (76), leading to rapid glucose spikes.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High saturated fat, trans fat, cholesterol, and deep-fried preparation
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: This food is not recommended for individuals with cardiovascular disease due to its high saturated fat (11.4g/100g), trans fat (0.43g/100g), and cholesterol (28.57mg/100g), exacerbated by deep-frying which can increase harmful compounds.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Matcha Mochimochi is a Japanese-inspired donut, popular in Singapore, characterized by its distinctive chewy, elastic texture, similar to mochi. It is typically deep-fried and then coated in a sweet frosting flavored with matcha (Japanese green tea powder). The donut's unique texture comes from the use of glutinous rice flour in combination with wheat flour. Nutritionally, it is an ultra-processed food, very high in sugar and fat, particularly saturated fat, and provides minimal dietary fiber. Its deep-fried nature and high sugar content make it a high glycemic index food, posing significant concerns for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, and cardiovascular disease. While matcha offers some antioxidants, its benefits are largely outweighed by the overall nutritional profile of this dessert.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from a similar food (cake-type donut) from USDA FoodData Central, with adjustments for specific ingredients like matcha and frosting. GI is an analogous estimate. Local names and aliases are based on common usage and translation.
notes: Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, potassium, vitamin C, vitamin A, vitamin B1, vitamin B2, vitamin B3, vitamin B6, vitamin B12, folate, vitamin D, vitamin E, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, selenium, and water values are estimated based on similar food items and general nutritional profiles for donuts, as specific data for 'Matcha Mochimochi' is not readily available in approved primary sources. The original input provided limited micronutrient data.
